b. Sample  
The (much smaller) group of those children whose ages are actually recorded and used in the study—for example, the 250 first-time students enrolled this winter at ten randomly chosen resorts.

The actual observed values of X for the children in the sample—for example:

o3 Answer:
Situation  
A marriage counselor wants to know what fraction of the people she has counseled eventually remain married.

a. Population  
All of the counselor’s clients (past, present, and future, or over whatever time span she is interested in).

b. Sample  
The particular group of clients on whom she actually gathers follow-up information—for example, the 120 couples she counseled during the last two years whom she was able to contact.

c. Parameter  
The true (but unknown) proportion of the entire population of her clients who stay married.

d. Statistic  
The proportion calculated from the sample.  Example: 93 of the 120 contacted couples are still married, so the statistic (sample proportion) is 93⁄120 = 0.775.

e. Variable  
For each client (or couple) the outcome “stayed married?” This is a categorical (binary) variable with possible values Yes or No (often coded 1 = Yes, 0 = No).

f. Data  
The individual observed values of the variable for every member of the sample.  Example:  
Yes, Yes, No, Yes, … (120 values in all), from which the statistic 0.775 was computed.

*   **Identification:** The parameter is the **true proportion of all potential consumers** in the population who will buy the product.
*   **Example:** The true, but unknown, percentage of *all* people in North America aged 13-40 who will buy the new video game console. We might represent this with the variable *p*.

### **d. The Statistic**
The statistic is a numerical value that describes a characteristic of the **sample**. It is calculated from the sample data and is used to estimate the population parameter.

*   **Identification:** The statistic is the **proportion of people in the sample** who state they will buy the product.
*   **Example:** If, out of the 2,000 people surveyed, 350 say they will buy the console, the statistic is the sample proportion: 350 / 2,000 = 0.175 or 17.5%. This statistic (17.5%) is the company's best estimate of the true population parameter. We might represent this with the variable *p̂* (p-hat).

### **e. The Variable**
The variable is the characteristic or measurement that is being recorded for each individual in the sample.

*   **Identification:** The variable, which we can call *X*, is the response of an individual to the question of whether they will buy the product.
*   **Example:** The variable is the answer each person gives. This is a categorical variable, as the possible values are not numbers. For example, the values could be "Yes, I will buy it" or "No, I will not buy it."

### **f. The Data**
The data are the actual values or responses of the variable collected from each individual in the sample.

*   **Identification:** The data are the collected "yes" or "no" answers from each person who was surveyed.
*   **Example:** The data would be a list of 2,000 individual responses, such as: {Yes, Yes, No, No, Yes, No, ...}. The collection of all these answers is the data set. From this data, the statistic (17.5%) is calculated.

c. Parameter: The true population proportion p of people in the target market who would buy the product.

Common problems that keep a mailed questionnaire from giving accurate, representative information include  

• Low response rate – many recipients simply throw the survey away or forget to send it back.  
• Non-response bias – the kinds of people who do mail it back can differ systematically (age, education, interest in the park, free time, etc.) from those who do not, so the answers are not representative of all residents.  
• Wrong or outdated addresses – the survey may never reach the intended household or the current occupant may not match the sampling frame.  
• Incomplete or illegible answers – without an interviewer present respondents may skip items, misunderstand wording, or write unusable responses.  
• Language or literacy barriers – some recipients may be unable to read or comfortably answer the survey.  
• Lack of opportunity for clarification – if a question is confusing the respondent cannot ask follow-up questions, increasing measurement error.  
• Mailing delays or lost returns – questionnaires can be delayed or lost in the postal system, further lowering the usable sample size.  
• Cost/hassle of returning – if postage is not prepaid, or even when it is, some people will not bother to mail it back.

2. Ecological correlation / ecological fallacy  
   • Aggregating to the state level can hide very different relationships at the individual level.  
   • Even if better‐educated individuals in every city actually commit less crime, you can still get a positive state‐level slope if big cities (with more crime) also happen to have more highly educated residents.  

3. Influence of outliers

   • Dropping or down‐weighting those outliers often makes the apparent relationship vanish or reverse.  

4. Correlation ≠ causation

   • Without a controlled experiment or a good strategy for removing confounding, you cannot draw any causal conclusions.

o3 Answer:
a)  Reasons the cooperation rate in telephone (or other) surveys has been falling  
•  Caller-ID, voice mail, and call-blocking allow people to screen unfamiliar numbers.  
•  Growth of telemarketing, “robocalls,” and scams has made the public wary of anyone who telephones asking questions.  
•  Many households no longer have a land-line; RDD frames miss, or must make special (and costly) arrangements to reach, cell-only users.  
•  Mobile phones are considered personal devices; users are less willing to talk to strangers and may incur air-time charges.  
•  Busy, two-earner and single-parent households leave less discretionary time to take a lengthy survey.  
•  Privacy and identity-theft concerns discourage giving information to unknown interviewers.  
•  Repeated requests from many polling organizations create “survey fatigue.”  
•  Cultural/language diversity can make contact and cooperation harder unless multilingual interviewers are available.  

b)  Why declining response rates worry survey researchers  
Random-digit-dial telephone surveys rely on the assumption that the people who do respond are essentially similar to the people who do not.  As the response rate falls, that assumption becomes more tenuous.  If those who refuse (or cannot be reached) differ systematically from those who participate—by age, income, political interest, education, race, etc.—the resulting estimates are biased.  This is called non-response bias.  Unlike ordinary sampling error (reflected in the “±3 % margin of error”), non-response bias does not shrink just by increasing the number of completed interviews; it can distort results no matter how large the final sample is.  Weighting adjustments help, but only for variables that are measured or known; unknown differences remain uncorrected.  Consequently, a falling response rate undermines the representativeness, accuracy, and credibility of public-opinion polls and any decisions or inferences based on them.

Several factors contribute to the decline in response rates for surveys, particularly in random digit dial (RDD) telephone surveys:

6. **Decline in Landline Usage** – Traditional RDD surveys relied on landlines, but fewer households maintain them, reducing the reachable population.  

### **b) Why Researchers Are Concerned About Declining Response Rates**  

Researchers worry about declining response rates because they can introduce **bias** and **reduce the validity** of survey results:  

1. **Nonresponse Bias** – If certain groups (e.g., younger people, minorities, or those with strong opinions) are less likely to respond, the sample may not represent the population accurately.  
2. **Reduced Generalizability** – Lower response rates mean the sample may skew toward those more willing to participate (e.g., older adults or individuals with strong political views), leading to misleading conclusions.  
3. **Threat to Survey Accuracy** – If response rates drop too low, even well-designed surveys may produce unreliable estimates, affecting policy decisions, marketing strategies, and public opinion tracking.  
4. **Increased Costs** – Researchers must make more attempts to reach respondents, raising expenses without guaranteeing better data quality.  

### **Conclusion**  
The decline in response rates challenges the reliability of public opinion polls. Without adjustments (e.g., weighting, mixed-mode surveys, or incentives), surveys risk producing biased results that misrepresent true population attitudes. Researchers must adapt methodologies to maintain accuracy despite these trends.
